title: King's Indian Attack
summary: Reversed King's Indian — a White system

steps.title: A Flexible Weapon
steps.text:
The **King's Indian Attack** (KIA) is the King's Indian Defense — *played by White*. Same setup, same plans, just colors reversed: White fianchettoes the king's bishop, castles short, and then unleashes a slow, methodical kingside attack.

It was Bobby Fischer's secret weapon when he wanted to dodge mainstream theory. Even today, top players like **Hikaru Nakamura** and **Levon Aronian** trot it out as a surprise weapon.

## The defining sequence

A model order is {{Nf3}} **{{d5}}** {{g3}} **{{Nf6}}** {{Bg2}} **{{c5}}** **{{O-O}}** **{{Nc6}}** {{d3}} **{{e6}}** {{Nbd2}}. The key signature is the cluster: **Nf3, g3, Bg2, 0-0, d3, Nbd2, e4** — almost regardless of what Black does.

## What each side wants

- **White's plan:** complete the fianchetto setup, then push **{{e4}}** and eventually **{{e5}}**, opening lines toward the kingside. Pieces stack up: **Re1**, **Nf1-g3**, **h2-h4-h5**, and a kingside breakthrough.
- **Black's plan:** treat it like a French Defense or Sicilian — develop solidly, contest the queenside (**...b5, ...c5-c4, ...Rb8**), and exploit White's slow setup.
- **Pawn structure:** *KIA / French structure* — White pawns at d3-e4 vs Black at d5-e6, with attacks on opposite wings.

## Why it's grandmaster-friendly

The KIA is **one system against everything** — Sicilian, French, Caro-Kann all transpose into similar middlegames. You learn one set of plans and apply it for life.

> The KIA is chess's universal answer. Slow setup, sharp attack.

steps.prompt: Start with the most flexible first move: **{{Nf3}}**.
steps.hint: 1.Nf3 develops a piece and keeps every option open. It can transpose to Reti, English, Catalan, or — as here — the KIA.

steps.prompt: Begin the fianchetto with **{{g3}}** — preparing the bishop's long diagonal.
steps.hint: g3 prepares the fianchetto — the bishop will live on g2, eyeing the long h1-a8 diagonal.

steps.title: KIA Structure
steps.text:
After **{{Nf3}}** and **{{g3}}**, the rest of the King's Indian Attack falls into a familiar groove.

## The full setup

1. **{{Nf3}}** — flexible knight development.
2. **{{g3}}** — preparing the fianchetto.
3. **{{Bg2}}** — bishop to its long-diagonal home.
4. **{{O-O}}** — castle short, king tucked away.
5. **{{d3}}** — solid central support.
6. **{{Nbd2}}** — second knight (not on c3, where it would block c-pawn).
7. **{{e4}}** — *the* attacking move. The center awakens.

From there: **Re1**, **Nf1-h2** or **Nf1-g3**, **h2-h4-h5**, and a kingside avalanche.

## A model game plan

After Black's typical setup (**...d5, ...Nf6, ...e6, ...c5, ...Nc6, ...Be7**), White breaks with **e4-e5** kicking the f6 knight. Then **Nf1-g3** and **h4-h5** open lines. Combined with **Qe2** and **Bf4**, the attack on f7/h7 becomes deadly.

## The KIA's main appeal

- **One opening fits all.** Sicilian? French? Caro-Kann? You play the KIA against all three.
- **Strategically rich.** The middlegame plans repeat, but the tactics are fresh every game.
- **Hard to refute.** Even strong opponents have to be careful — Black's natural setups walk into White's plans.

> If you want one weapon for life, learn the KIA. You'll never be without an answer.
